articles.title: The BigHand Legal Report: More Trouble For Ostriches
articles.content:
我最近报道了德勤法律（Deloitte Legal）的一项调查结果，该调查揭示了法务部门在人工智能方面与律师事务所之间持续存在的巨大差距。总体而言，法务部门在人工智能的使用和期望方面遥遥领先于其合作的律所。法务部门代表认为，外部律师完成的相当一部分工作将被内部消化，人工智能的使用将会增加。德勤认为，律师事务所在很大程度上还没有做好准备。

在我的帖子发布后不久，我发现了另一项研究，这次来自一家供应商，从略有不同的角度得出了相同的结论。这项名为《法律工作流程领导力报告》的研究和报告由BigHand完成，这是一家专注于律师事务所业务端的法律科技供应商。该报告基于800多位律师事务所领导者和法律专业人士的洞察。

从阅读BigHand报告中可以清楚地看出，这项研究的目的是说服律师事务所购买BigHand的产品。因此，我总是对这些调查持保留态度。但考虑到德勤法律从法务部门角度得出的发现，BigHand研究的可信度提高了。综合来看，对律师事务所来说前景并不乐观。

一个可怕的一致性

事实上，BigHand的一些结论非常准确，因为它们与德勤法律在律师事务所方面的发现高度一致。BigHand指出：

“今年的发现……揭示了人工智能时代运营准备方面日益扩大的差距。虽然现在几乎所有律所都在某种程度上在支持服务中使用人工智能，但正在重新设计工作流程、授权模式、角色和技能结构以有效支持人工智能的律所却少得多。”

当然，这项调查专注于支持服务。这是律师事务所的业务端服务，而非德勤法律关注的面向客户的法律工作。但如果律师事务所在可以降低内部成本的支持端都难以做到正确，他们还能指望在法律工作端做什么呢？人工智能在那里有降低收入的潜力。

考虑一下BigHand的这项发现：“支持团队的利用率往往不明确，律所在衡量人工智能对生产力、盈利能力和客户服务真正影响方面存在困难。”德勤法律报告也注意到了大致相同的情况：人工智能迫使法律服务的定价和估值方式发生变化，但太多律师事务所不知道如何做出这种改变，也没有这种能力。

客户将用脚投票

对于那些不愿意改变处理和使用人工智能方式的律师事务所来说，实际调查结果描绘了一幅严峻的画面。95%的受调查律所报告客户流失增加。几乎一半的律所表示，客户正在质疑为什么尽管人工智能已经可用，但费用却没有下降。42%的律所表示，客户正在要求替代性收费安排。

与德勤法律一样，BigHand得出结论：客户期望正在提高，更多工作将被内部消化，法务部门正在减少推荐律师名单上的合作律所数量。客户希望更快的周转、更高的透明度和更高质量的服务交付。那些不满足这些日益增长的需求和期望的律所将发现自己被取代，而且往往没有任何预警。

支持人员的当前使用情况

BigHand认为，在人工智能时代，满足客户期望在很大程度上取决于支持人员的管理和授权。这是有道理的：以我的经验，如果你没有一个糟糕的支持团队，你就无法以良好的价格提供顶级服务。

然而，近一半的受访者表示，他们预计在不久的将来将失去21%至50%的支持人员。他们承认，这种人员流失将直接影响计费工时、收入，也许最重要的是，会扰乱客户关系。这种关系一旦被破坏就很难修复。这种中断可能带来长期财务甚至生存方面的后果。

尽管预计支持人员会流失，但只有21%的律所正在使用BigHand所谓的结构化工作流程技术来更好地分配任务和改进授权。BigHand直截了当地指出：

“如果没有对工作量、产能和绩效清晰且基于实证的理解，就很难确保正确的工作由正确的资源处理——而人工智能对生产力和盈利能力真正影响几乎不可能衡量。”

另一个有趣的发现：43%的受访者表示，现在需要额外时间来检查和验证人工智能输出。换句话说，由于律所还没有找到高效准确地验证输出的方法，人工智能使用节省的大量时间——这些本可以回馈给客户的节省——被抵消了。当然，验证至关重要。但BigHand的观点很有道理。许多律所还没有弄清楚如何高效地做到这一点。

最后，只有23%的律所在开发报告技术，以解释人工智能效率如何应用于客户工作。难怪在德勤法律调查的受访者中，只有4%表示他们的律所向他们展示了人工智能使用的任何好处。坦率地说，这些律所毫无头绪。

那么律所如何应对人员流失呢？

简短的答案是：他们没有。近20%计划将现有员工薪资涨幅控制在5%以下；不到40%计划涨幅仅在5-10%范围内。只有27%的律所认为优先考虑律师如何向支持人员传递工作很重要。只有64%使用技术来追踪支持人员的工作效率和生产力。BigHand指出，没有数据，律所怎么可能真正确定他们需要什么员工以及如何部署这些员工。只有29%的律所对其支持团队进行了调整以纳入新技能。

还有这个：只有41%的律所审查了支持人员任务，以确定自动化和人工智能可以在哪里产生最大影响。

为什么如此惨淡？

为什么数字如此惨淡？BigHand没有发表意见，但我会。基于经验，这是律师的傲慢，就是这么简单。律师们用蔑视的眼光看待他们所谓的在律所工作的“非律师”。太多人仍然认为，即使小声说，只有律师才能有效地经营和管理律所。只有权益合伙人才能做出商业和支持人员决策。合伙人说，毕竟，我们拥有律所，我们经营律所，而你们，支持人员，为我们工作。这意味着支持团队中的法律专业人员被忽视和低估。

毫无疑问，已经取得了进展。许多律师事务所现在有了首席运营官和首席财务官，而不是由拥有最大业务量的合伙人经营律所。但旧习惯很难改变，尤其是在许多律所仍然采用的共识决策模式下。

那么我们现在处于什么位置？

通常，我会警告不要对供应商的调查给予过多信任——这些调查恰好显示律所需求，而供应商产品恰好能满足这些需求。但在这个案例中，由于发现与德勤法律的发现如此一致，这些数字揭示了一种持续存在的、令人担忧的“把头埋在沙子里”的文化。这种文化更令人担忧，因为正如BigHand所说，“在客户和律师之间，权力平衡已经转移。”这是德勤法律注意到的一个趋势，也是我之前报道过的。

底线是什么？那些把头埋在沙子里的鸵鸟律师事务所将失去业务。德勤法律看到了这一点。BigHand看到了这一点。在大型律师事务所工作了30多年后，我也看到了这一点。

把头从沙子里拿出来。

斯蒂芬·恩布里（Stephen Embry）是一位律师、演讲者、博主和作家。他发布TechLaw Crossroads，这是一个致力于审视技术、法律与法律执业之间紧张关系的博客。

这篇文章《BigHand法律报告：鸵鸟们的更多麻烦》首次发表于Above the Law。
articles.summary:
I recently reported on the findings of a Deloitte Legal survey that revealed the continuing sizable gap between where legal departments are with respect to AI versus their law firms. Broadly put, legal departments are well ahead of their firms when it comes to AI use and expectations. Legal department representatives believe a good portion of work done by outside lawyers will be insourced and AI usage will increase. It was Deloitte’s view that law firms are by and large not ready.

Shortly after my post, I came across another study, this time by a vendor, that suggests the same thing albeit from a slightly different angle. The study and report, entitled Legal Workflow Leadership Report was done by BigHand, a legal tech vendor that focuses on the business side of law practice. It drew on the insights from over 800 law firm leaders and legal professionals.

From reading the BigHand report, it’s clear the goal of the study was to help convince law firms to purchase BigHand’s products. As such, I always take these kinds of surveys with a grain of salt. But with the Deloitte Legal findings from the legal department side, the credibility of the BigHand study goes up. And taken together, the pictures aren’t pretty for law firms.

A Scary Consistency

Indeed, some of the BigHand conclusions were spot on since they were so consistent with what Deloitte Legal found when it came to law firms. Says BigHand:

This year’s findings…reveal a growing gap in operational readiness for the AI age. While almost all firms are now using AI across support services in some capacity, far fewer are redesigning workflow, delegation models, roles and skill structures needed to support it effectively.

The survey, of course, focused on support services. These are services on the business side of the law firms and not legal work on the client side on which Deloitte Legal focused. But if law firms are struggling to get it right on the support side which can lower their internal costs, what can they hope to do on the legal work side where AI has the propensity to reduce revenues?

Consider this finding from BigHand: “support team utilization often remains unclear, and firms struggle to measure the true impact of AI on productivity, profitability and client service.” The Deloitte Legal report noted much the same thing: AI compels a change in how legal services are priced and valued but too many law firms have no idea how to make that change. Nor the ability.

Clients Will Vote with Their Feet

The actual findings paint a grim picture for law firms that aren’t willing to change how they deal with and use AI. Ninety-five percent of the firms surveyed already report increased client attrition. Almost half say their clients are wondering why fees have not decreased despite the availability of AI. Forty-two percent say clients are asking for alternative fee arrangements.

And like Deloitte Legal, BigHand concludes client expectations are going up, more work will be brought in-house, and in-house legal is reducing the number of panel counsel. Clients want faster turnaround, more transparency, and higher quality delivery. Firms that don’t meet these increasing desires and expectations will find themselves replaced, often without a word of warning.

The Current Use of Support Staff

BigHand believes that meeting client expectations in the age of AI hinges in large part on support staff management and delegation. It stands to reason: in my experience, you can’t deliver top-notch service at a good price with a piss poor support staff.

Yet almost half of those surveyed say they expect to lose anywhere between 21 to 50% of their support staff in the near future. And they admit this attrition will have a direct impact on billable hours, revenue and, perhaps most importantly, disrupt client relationships. Client relationships that are hard to repair once disrupted. That disruption can bring long-term financial and even survivability consequences.

Despite these expected losses to support staff, only 21% are using what BigHand calls structured workflow technology to better allocate tasks and improve delegation. BigHand puts it bluntly:

Without a clear and empirical understanding of workload, capacity and performance, it is difficult to ensure that the right work is handled by the right resource—and the true impact of AI on productivity and profitability is almost impossible to measure.

Another interesting finding: 43% of those surveyed say additional time is now required to check and verify AI outputs. In other words, because firms have not figured out a way to efficiently and accurately verify outputs, much of the time savings from the use of AI, savings that could be passed along to clients, is offset. Certainly, verification is critical. But BigHand’s point is a good one. A lot of firms have not yet figured out how to do that efficiently.

Finally, only 23% are developing reporting techniques to explain how AI efficiencies are being applied to client work. No wonder only 4% of those surveyed by Deloitte Legal say their firms have shown them any sort of benefits from the use of AI. Frankly, the firms are clueless.

And How Are Firms Dealing with the Attrition?

Short answer: they aren’t. Close to 20% plan increase in salaries to existing staff of less than 5%; less than 40% plan increases only in the 5-10% range. Only 27% think prioritizing how lawyers pass work to support staff is important. Only 64% use technology to track support staff productivity and efficiency. Without data, how in the world can firms determine what staff they really need and how to deploy it, says BigHand. Only 29% have made changes to their support teams to incorporate new skill sets.

And get this one: only 41% have reviewed support staff tasks to determine where automation and AI can deliver the greatest impact.

Why So Dismal?

Why are the numbers so dismal? BigHand doesn’t opine but I will. Based on experience, it’s lawyer hubris, pure and simple. Lawyers look down their noses at what they call the “non-lawyers” working in a firm. Too many still think even if under their breath that only the lawyers can effectively run and manage the firm. That only the equity partners can make business and support staff decisions. After all, say the partners, we own the firm, we run the firm, and you, support staff, work for us. Which means the legal professionals on support staffs are ignored and undervalued.

Unquestionably, strides have been made. Lots of law firms now have COOs and CFOs instead of having the firm run by the partner with the biggest book of business. But old habits die hard, especially with a consensus-based decision model which many firms still have.

So Where Are We?

Ordinarily, I would caution placing too much stock in a vendor’s survey that coincidentally shows law firm needs that vendor products happen to meet. But in this case, where the findings are so consistent with those of Deloitte Legal, the numbers reveal a continuing and alarming “hide your head in the sand” culture. A culture that’s even more concerning since, as between the clients and the lawyers, “the balance of power has shifted,” says BigHand. That’s a trend noted by Deloitte Legal and which I have reported on before.

Bottom line? The ostrich law firms that keep their heads in the sand are going to lose business. Deloitte Legal sees it. BigHand sees it. And after over 30 years in Biglaw, I see it.

Get your head out of the sand.

Stephen Embry is a lawyer, speaker, blogger, and writer. He publishes TechLaw Crossroads, a blog devoted to the examination of the tension between technology, the law, and the practice of law.

The post The BigHand Legal Report: More Trouble For Ostriches appeared first on Above the Law.

articles.title: The DOJ’s Biglaw Subpoena Explanation Raises More Questions Than It Answers
articles.content:
还记得特朗普对那九家屈服的Biglaw律所翻脸的事情吗？这些律所为逃避他那些具有宪法争议的报复性行政令，出卖了法治原则。现在我们有更多细节了解这一决定背后的原因。

简单回顾一下：司法部向这九家律所发出第三方传票，要求提供他们与特朗普私人律师鲍里斯·埃普斯特恩之间通讯记录，同时对签署协议的律所领导人发出取证通知——这一切都是司法部与美国律师协会（ABA）法律纠纷的附带效应。美国律师协会起诉了本届政府通过行政令进行法律黑名单的运动。

彭博法律报道，当被问及对传票的评论时，一位匿名司法部发言人实际上这样回答：

“我们向律所发出传票，是为了强化我们的论点，即美国律师协会应该从其会员那里获取这些信息，而不是从一个与总统关系密切的顾问那里。”

哇哦。我对此有一些看法。

好的，司法部在正在进行的证据开示斗争中向记者解释其诉讼策略，这确实很奇怪。律师们通常不会在传票墨迹未干时就透露发出传票的“为什么”——这类内容通常是留到法律简状中，而不是媒体询问时。更值得记住的是，由于这九家律所尚未正式反对这些传票，它们仍然担心反抗会招致另一份针对它们的行政令，因此司法部本可以暂时将这张牌藏得更紧一些。

同样值得注意的是，这个理论本身也站不住脚。当然，达成价值9.4亿美元公益回馈协议的九家律所可能有一些相关文件。但即便如此，“一些”也不等于“全部”。美国律师协会不仅仅是在询问这九项协议的律所一方，它特别是在询问白宫和埃普斯特恩在策划所有这些协议中所扮演的角色。律所不会在这些沟通中被抄送。

而且，这难道不是在承认埃普斯特恩通讯材料的相关性吗？这些正是本届政府正在努力阻止美国律师协会获取的材料！但是，通过向律所发出传票索取其与埃普斯特恩的通讯记录，司法部刚刚承认了，无论埃普斯特恩对行政令说了什么，都与美国律师协会的案件相关。现在，不可否认，政府正在对这些材料主张律师-当事人特权，正如发言人所说，“司法部驳回美国律师协会对埃普斯特恩相关材料的传票的动议，符合保护任何总统获得最亲密顾问保密建议的长期做法。”看来，在他们不必这样做的时候承认相关性，是一个非受迫性失误。

因此，美国律师协会的证据开示之争刚刚收到了一份礼物——来自司法部自己的传票，以及它自己的新闻办公室。

此前：特朗普对屈服的Biglaw律所翻脸 哈哈哈哈

美国律师协会想要白宫关于Biglaw行政令的证据

特朗普政府为Biglaw行政令提出的最佳论点是你必须相信总统

司法部为特朗普Biglaw行政令辩护：看看我们吓倒了多少律所！

司法部放弃为Biglaw行政令辩护，让屈服的律所背负9.4亿美元的重担

起诉政府后向特朗普屈膝的Biglaw律所没有免罪

“祈祷我不要再进一步修改”：达斯·维达应该教律所什么关于与特朗普和解的道理

凯瑟琳·鲁比诺是Above the Law的高级编辑、The Jabot播客主持人，以及Thinking Like A Lawyer的联合主持人。AtL的线人是最好的，所以请关注她。随时给她发邮件提供任何线索、问题或评论，并在Twitter @Kathryn1或Bluesky @Kathryn1上关注她。这篇文章《司法部关于Biglaw传票的解释提出的问题比回答的更多》首次发表于Above the Law。
articles.summary:
Remember when Trump turned on the nine capitulating Biglaw firms that sold out the rule of law to escape his retaliatory, constitutionally dubious executive orders? Yeah, well, now we’ve got more details on what’s behind that decision.

Quick recap for anyone just catching up: DOJ hit the nine firms with third-party subpoenas demanding their communications with Trump’s personal lawyer Boris Epshteyn, plus deposition notices for the firm leaders who actually signed those deals — all as a side effect of its legal tussle with the ABA, which sued the administration over the legal-blacklist-by-executive-order campaign.

Bloomberg Law reports that when asked for comment on the subpoenas, an unnamed DOJ spokesperson actually answered:

“We sent the subpoenas to the law firms to reinforce our argument that the ABA should obtain this information from its own members, rather than a close presidential advisor.”

Whoa, doggie. I have some thoughts about that.

Okay, it is genuinely strange that DOJ is narrating its own litigation strategy to a reporter in the middle of a live discovery fight. Attorneys are not exactly known for offering up the “why” behind a subpoena while the ink is still wet… that’s usually the kind of thing you save for a brief, not a press inquiry. Worth remembering, too, that since the nine firms haven’t formally opposed these subpoenas yet, still too worried that fighting back will earn them another executive order for their trouble, the DOJ could have kept this card closer to their chest, at least for the moment.

Also it is worth noting the theory doesn’t hold up on its own terms. Sure, the nine firms that cut deals worth $940 million in pro bono payola may have some responsive documents. But, even if they do, “some” is not “the entire universe.” The ABA isn’t just asking about the firms’ side of nine deals, it’s specifically asking about the White House’s and Epshteyn’s role in orchestrating all of them. The law firms wouldn’t have been cc’d on that.

And isn’t this just conceding the responsiveness of the Epshteyn communications? Those are the exact materials the administration is fighting to keep out of the hands of the ABA! But, by subpoenaing the firms for their Epshteyn communications, the DOJ just admitted that whatever Epshteyn said about the EOs is relevant to the ABA case. Now, admittedly, the administration is making a privilege claim over the materials, as the spokesperson said, “DOJ’s motion to quash [the ABA’s subpoena of materials related to Epshteyn] is consistent with longstanding practice to protect any president’s ability to obtain confidential advice from his closest advisors.” It seems an unforced error to concede responsiveness when they didn’t have to.

So the ABA’s discovery fight just got a gift, courtesy of the department’s own subpoenas, and its own press office.

Earlier: Trump Turns On Capitulating Biglaw Firms HAHAHAHAHA
The ABA Wants The White House’s Receipts On The Biglaw Executive Orders
The Trump Administration’s Best Argument For Its Biglaw EOs Is That You Just Have To Trust The President
DOJ’s Defense Of Trump’s Biglaw Executive Orders: Look How Many Firms We Scared Into Compliance!
DOJ Drops Defense Of Biglaw Executive Orders, Leaving Capitulating Firms Holding $940 Million Bag
There’s No Absolution For Biglaw Firms Suing The Administration After Bending A Knee To Trump
‘Pray I Don’t Alter It Any Further’: What Darth Vader Should Teach Law Firms About Settling With Trump

Kathryn Rubino is a Senior Editor at Above the Law, host of The Jabot podcast, and co-host of Thinking Like A Lawyer. AtL tipsters are the best, so please connect with her. Feel free to email her with any tips, questions, or comments and follow her on Twitter @Kathryn1 or Bluesky @Kathryn1

The post The DOJ’s Biglaw Subpoena Explanation Raises More Questions Than It Answers appeared first on Above the Law.

articles.title: Veteran GOP Election Lawyer Still Has One Problem With Trump’s 2020 Claims: The Evidence
articles.content:
编者按：欢迎阅读我们的每日栏目“每日名言”。

让我印象深刻的是，目前仍然没有任何证据表明任何选举结果是错误的。仍然没有文件，仍然没有证据，尽管我们拭目以待最终会公布什么。

——本·金斯伯格，帕顿·博格斯和琼斯·戴律师事务所的退休合伙人，曾担任多位共和党总统竞选活动的全国法律顾问。上述言论是在唐纳德·特朗普就所谓的选民欺诈和2020年选举“操纵”问题发表全国讲话后发表的。

斯蒂西·扎雷茨基是Above the Law的编辑总监，自2011年起在该网站工作。她非常欢迎您的来信，欢迎通过电子邮件发送任何线索、问题、评论或批评。您可以在Bluesky、X/Twitter和Threads上关注她，或在LinkedIn上与她联系。

这篇《资深共和党选举律师对特朗普2020年主张仍有疑问：证据在哪里》最先发布于Above the Law。
articles.summary:
Ed. note: Welcome to our daily feature, Quote of the Day.

What stood out to me is there is still no evidence of the result of any election being incorrect. There still were not the documents, there still was not the evidence, although we’ll see what’s produced.

—  Ben Ginsberg, a retired partner at Patton Boggs and Jones Day who has served as national counsel to several Republican presidential campaigns, in comments given in the wake of Donald Trump’s national address concerning alleged voter fraud and the so-called rigging of the 2020 election.

Staci Zaretsky is the managing editor of Above the Law, where she’s worked since 2011. She’d love to hear from you, so please feel free to email her with any tips, questions, comments, or critiques. You can follow her on Bluesky, X/Twitter, and Threads, or connect with her on LinkedIn.

The post Veteran GOP Election Lawyer Still Has One Problem With Trump’s 2020 Claims: The Evidence appeared first on Above the Law.

articles.title: Litigants Ask To Check Reflecting Pool For ‘Sabotage’
articles.content:
总统花了一晚上谈论六年前的选举，醒来后又谈论林肯纪念堂倒影池。

这个隐喻再次暴露腐败。泳池再次被排空修复"美国国旗蓝"衬层，该衬层在特朗普的人安装后不久就开始脱落。倾倒的过氧化氢没解决藻类问题，总统车队在环氧树脂固化时从上面驶过也造成损害。

总统坚称泳池遭共产主义破坏分子蓄意破坏，这些破坏者似乎拥有魔力，能躲避全天候监控的多个摄像头。

"切痕长达300码，泳池底部被切开，被暴徒用大力向上撕裂，"他尖叫道。"这些憎恨国家的卑鄙小人应付出代价。让美国再次伟大！"

目前泳池已围栏隔离。联邦调查局取证团队周三前往现场，试图搜集证据支持总统说法，但根据《华盛顿邮报》调查，损害与环氧树脂接缝高度吻合，延伸至泳池中心。

该死，那些忍者真狡猾！

7月2日，哥伦比亚特区联邦检察官珍妮娜·皮罗宣布抓获一名罪魁祸首戴维·赫恩，此人曾是奥运皮划艇选手——跟忍者差不多！他在哥伦比亚特区高等法院被控重罪破坏罪，肯定会要求查看其所谓造成损害的证据。同时还有一桩悬而未决的民事诉讼，原告也要求检查泳池。

文化景观基金会的诉讼主要关于文书工作——国家公园管理局在改造国家纪念碑前没完成的文书工作。基金会春季起诉内政部，认为国家公园管理局跳过了历史保护审查和环境审查，只为赶在特朗普7月4日派对前将灰色池底变成蓝色。政府称1500万美元拆除百年防水系统是紧急维修或常规替换，无需审查。国家公园管理局运营副主任弗兰克·兰兹的宣誓声明未重复特朗普"300码"切痕的说法。他指出公园警察回应了倒影池损坏报告，涉及密封胶被刀片割开，以及分层表面材料损坏。兰兹似乎承认衬层在破坏者出现前已在"分层"。文化景观基金会决心查清！

原告援引"收集和保存证据的独特临时机会"，请求卡尔·尼科尔斯法官"允许原告律师团队成员临时进入倒影池周围人行道，拍摄和检查池底状况"。

文化景观基金会认为政府已就泳池状况做出陈述，一旦注满水和藻类，证据就消失。《联邦民事诉讼规则》第34条允许法院下令现场检查，华盛顿特区联邦法官在证据可能消失时会批准此类请求。原告必须获取自己的证据，在被告永久改变和销毁证据前。

特朗普政府计划显然是事后寻求宽恕，而非事先获得许可。这招在白宫东翼似乎成功了，但这里政府的能力不足可能绊倒它，因为"分层"的倒影池已是既成事实。

莉兹·戴制作《法律与混乱》Substack和播客。点击标志订阅：
本文首次发布于Above the Law。
articles.summary:
After spending the evening free associating blather about a six-year-old election, the president woke up and started free associating blather about the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool.

The universe’s least subtle metaphor is once again revealing its filthy, corrupted interior. The monument was drained again to repair the “American flag blue” lining which started to visibly separate from the basin shortly after Trump’s pool guys rolled it on — and the hydrogen peroxide they dumped in there to kill the algae bloom didn’t help. Ditto for the presidential motorcade driving over it as the epoxy was curing.

The president insists the pool was sabotaged by communist wreckers, presumably ninjas with magical powers to evade the observation of the multiple cameras that are trained on the pool at all hours.

“The slashes were 300 yards long, and the floor of the pool was cut and then pulled upward, with great force, by these thugs,” he screeched. “These Country hating sleazebags should pay a big price for the damage done. MAKE AMERICA GREAT AGAIN!”

As of now, the pool is fenced off to the public. An FBI forensic team was out there on Wednesday trying to gin up evidence to support the president’s claims, but according to an investigative report by the Washington Post, the damage lines up pretty cleanly with seams in the epoxy and extends throughout the center of the pool.

Damn, those ninjas are tricky!

On July 2, US Attorney for DC Jeanine Pirro announced that they’d nabbed one of the culprits, David Hearn, a former Olympic canoeist — which is kind of like a ninja! He’s been charged with felony vandalism in DC Superior Court and will certainly seek discovery of the supposed damage he inflicted. But in the meantime there’s a pending civil suit where the plaintiffs are also demanding a better look at the pool.

Until this week, The Cultural Landscape Foundation’s lawsuit was mostly about paperwork — specifically, the paperwork the National Park Service didn’t do before it tarted up yet another national monument. TCLF sued the Department of the Interior in the spring, arguing that NPS blew through the historic-preservation review required before altering a National Register-listed landscape as well as the required environmental review, all so the pool’s grey basin could be turned blue in time for Donald Trump’s July 4th party. The government insists that the $15 million teardown of a century-old waterproofing system was either an emergency repair or a mundane substitution that it is exempt from review. Notably, a sworn declaration filed in the case by Frank Lands, NPS’s Deputy Director for Operations, does not repeat Trump’s claims about slashes that were “300 yards long.” Instead, Lands notes that “the U.S. Park Police responded to an NPS report of damage to the reflecting pool, including a caulk over the foam sealant that was cut with a sharp knife or razor and destruction of delaminating surface material.”

Was the NPS report accurate? Lands does not say, although he does seem to concede that the liner was already “delaminating” before any of the saboteurs showed up. But TCLF aims to find out!

Citing this “unique but temporary opportunity for Plaintiffs to collect and preserve evidence that may be relevant to this case,” the plaintiff asks Judge Carl Nichols to “allow members of Plaintiffs’ counsel team (including any investigators and/or experts) temporary access to the sidewalk surrounding the Reflecting Pool for the limited purpose of photographing and inspecting the condition of the basin to preserve that evidence for future use in the case.”

TCLF argues that the government has made representations about the condition of this pool, and once the basin is full of water and algae, whatever’s actually down there is gone. Rule 34 permits a court to order a site inspection, and federal judges in DC grant this kind of request routinely when there’s a real risk evidence might disappear or even be spoliated.

“Defendants are seemingly attempting to shift blame for the defects in the Pool’s basin from their own mishandling of the resurfacing project,” they continue. “Plaintiffs must be permitted to obtain their own evidence regarding Defendants’ factual assertions before Defendants permanently alter and spoliate that very evidence.”

The Trump administration’s plan was clearly to seek forgiveness after the fact, rather than permission in advance as required by statute. It appears to have worked with the East Wing of the White House. But here, its own incompetence may trip it up, since the “delaminating” Reflecting Pool is clearly a fait un-accompli.

Liz Dye produces the Law and Chaos Substack and podcast. You can subscribe by clicking the logo:

The post Litigants Ask To Check Reflecting Pool For ‘Sabotage’ appeared first on Above the Law.

articles.title: What Lawyers Really Need To Know About Marketing
articles.content:
本期节目中，我邀请到国内最资深的法律营销与业务发展顾问之一Deborah Farone，深入探讨真正推动律所可持续发展的动力所在。Farone回顾了她的职业生涯：从1990年代一家负责Milbank业务的公关公司，到Debevoise和Cravath担任首席营销官，再到如今经营自己的咨询顾问公司。

对话涵盖了大律所在业务开发方面的常见误区、为何现有客户是最被忽视的增长杠杆、律所如何终于开始教授以往秘而不宣的业务开发技能，以及女性律师以自己的方式开拓业务究竟意味着什么。Farone还谈到了行业整合浪潮、中型律所面临的挤压，以及当前任何律所面临的最大战略风险是停滞不前。

关键要点：

业务开发始于战略，而非战术。在选择任何工具或活动之前，先明确你要去哪里。

大多数业务来自现有客户。深化这些关系并获得他们的转介绍，比追逐新客户更有效。

“监督者/拉客户者/办案者”的模式已经过时。如今每位律师在各个级别都被期望以某种形式开发业务。

建立蓬勃事业的女性律师，往往以自己感到真实的方式做到这一点——歌剧晚宴、与客户徒步——而不是照搬别人的套路。

任何律所面临的最大风险是自满。首席法务官希望律所主动向他们提供情报和AI指导，而非相反。

Kathryn Rubino是Above the Law的高级编辑、The Jabot播客主持人，以及Thinking Like A Lawyer的联合主持人。AtL的线人是最棒的，请与她联系。欢迎通过电子邮件发送任何线索、问题或意见，并在Twitter @Kathryn1或Bluesky @Kathryn1上关注她。

这篇文章首次发布于Above the Law。
articles.summary:
In this episode, I sit down with Deborah Farone, one of the most experienced legal marketing and business development advisors in the country, to dig into what actually drives sustainable practice growth. Farone traces her career from a PR firm handling Milbank’s account in the 1990s through Chief Marketing Officer roles at Debevoise and Cravath, to running her own advisory practice today.

The conversation covers what Biglaw gets wrong about business development, why existing clients are the most overlooked growth lever, how law firms are finally starting to teach associates what was once left unspoken, and what it really means for women lawyers to develop business on their own terms. Farone also addresses the industry’s consolidation wave, the squeeze on midsize firms, and why the biggest strategic risk for any law firm right now is standing still.

Key Takeaways:

* Business development starts with strategy, not tactics. Know where you want to go before you pick any tool or activity.
* Most business comes from existing clients. Growing those relationships and getting referrals from them is more powerful than chasing new names.
* The minders/finders/grinders model is outdated. Every lawyer at every level is now expected to develop business in some form.
* Women who build thriving practices do it in ways that feel authentic to them — opera evenings, hikes with clients — not by mimicking someone else’s playbook.
* The biggest risk for any law firm is complacency. GCs want firms to come to them with intelligence and AI guidance, not the other way around.

Kathryn Rubino is a Senior Editor at Above the Law, host of The Jabot podcast, and co-host of Thinking Like A Lawyer. AtL tipsters are the best, so please connect with her. Feel free to email her with any tips, questions, or comments and follow her on Twitter @Kathryn1 or Bluesky @Kathryn1

The post What Lawyers Really Need To Know About Marketing appeared first on Above the Law.
